goal_13.config.title: Exposure, Vulnerability, and Adaptation to Climate Hazards
goal_13.config.abstract: Extreme weather events, such as floods, droughts, storms, and heatwaves occur more often and are stronger due to climate change and impact more than half the world’s population. Almost a third of those affected are also highly vulnerable and must adapt to a changing climate.
goal_13.config.citation: Thijs Benschop, David Groves, Jonas Helth Lønborg and Ændra Rininsland. 2026. “Exposure, vulnerability and adaptation to climate hazards” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by D. Mahler, A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO. https://www.doi.org/10.60616/
goal_13.config.credits: Thijs Benschop, Jonas Helth Lønborg, David Groves, Ændra Rininsland
goal_13.config.inputs: Jia Li, Esther Naikal, Minh Cong Nguyen
goal_13.config.homeFactoidText: people are exposed and vulnerable to climate hazards
goal_13.config.homeTagline: Climate change intensifies extreme weather events, exposing more than half the global population, a large share of which is also vulnerable and in need of adaptation.
goal_13.content.#0.text: Climate change affects people, livelihoods and the economy
goal_13.content.#1.text: Climate change affects natural and human systems as temperatures rise, rainfall patterns shift, and extreme weather events, such as heatwaves, floods, droughts, and storms, become more frequent and intense.[reference: IPCC2023] These changes disrupt ecosystems and economies alike. Prolonged droughts reduce agricultural yields,[reference: FAO1] floods destroy homes and infrastructure, and heat stress threatens health and productivity.[reference: ILO1]
goal_13.content.#2.text: These impacts are not felt equally. People in different areas face different levels of exposures to climate hazards. However, even when faced with the same hazard, some people are more likely to be adversely affected or unable to cope with the impacts, for instance due to living in poverty or lacking access to social protection.[reference: WB3]
goal_13.content.#3.text: Let’s see which climate hazards the world’s population is exposed to and what makes people vulnerable.
goal_13.content.#5.text: We focus on four climate-related hazards: [emphasis: floods],[emphasis: ][emphasis: agricultural droughts], [emphasis: cyclones][emphasis: ]and [emphasis: heatwaves]. Not all places are equally exposed to them and the number of people living in affected areas differs substantially. Therefore, to estimate the number of people per country who are exposed, we need to overlay the geographical location of the hazards with the number of people living there.
goal_13.content.#6.text: The population exposed is defined as those people living in an area affected by a given climate-related hazard. An area is considered exposed if the probability that a hazard strikes in a given year is more than one percent. This translates into a[emphasis:  ]probability of slightly over 50 percent[footnote: To estimate the exposure, a 100-year return period is considered, which is the same as a 1 percent probability that the hazard occurs in a given year. The average life expectancy in the World is 72 years. An event with a probability of annual occurrence of 0.01 has a probability of 1-(1-0.01)^72=0.515 or 52% to occur in a period of 72 years. ] that a person faces the hazard over their life span. Weather events are considered hazards when their intensity is high enough to significantly impact lives, assets and livelihoods.[footnote:  To determine whether a climate-related hazard has a significant impact hazard-specific thresholds are used. For instance, for floods a threshold is used for inundation depth and for cyclones a threshold for wind speed is used.]
goal_13.content.#7.title: Population exposed to selected climate-related hazards
goal_13.content.#7.label: globally in 2024[reference: WB1]
goal_13.content.#8.text: Let’s look at the people exposed to [emphasis: floods],[emphasis: ][emphasis: agricultural droughts], [emphasis: cyclones][emphasis: ]and [emphasis: heatwaves].
goal_13.content.#9.text: [emphasis: Floods] occur because of heavy rain, overflowing rivers and rising sea levels. Climate change affects precipitation patterns, snow and ice melt as well as sea level rise leading to more frequent and heavier floods.[reference: UNEP1]
goal_13.content.#10.title: Exposure to floods
goal_13.content.#10.subtitle: Area and share of population exposed to floods with inundation depth over 50 cm[footnote:  An area is defined as being exposed to floods if there is an annual probability of more than 1 percent to experience flooding with inundation depth exceeding 50 centimeters.]
goal_13.content.#10.source: Deltares, Fathom, Hill et al. [reference: WB2]
goal_13.content.#10.visdescription: Share of population exposed to floods and location of areas exposed to flood risk
goal_13.content.#10.scenes.#0.text: [emphasis: Floods] can severely damage infrastructure and homes, disrupt the economy, and destroy crops and livestock. Impacts become particularly severe when inundation depth exceeds 50 centimeters.[reference: Rentschler] Areas shown in [emphasis: blue] indicate exposure above this level.
goal_13.content.#10.scenes.#1.text: The [emphasis: location of the population exposed to ] [emphasis: floods] is closely tied to the location of rivers, land elevation and coastal proximity. For example, in The Netherlands more than a third of the population is exposed to [emphasis: floods] due to its many rivers and the low elevation.
goal_13.content.#10.scenes.#2.text: Globally, [emphasis: 775 million people are exposed to] [emphasis: floods.] Half of them live in three populous countries: China, India and Bangladesh. In Bangladesh, one third of the population is exposed to [emphasis: floods].
goal_13.content.#11.text: [emphasis: Agricultural drought] is a period when soil moisture and water availability become insufficient to meet the needs of crops and livestock resulting in reduced agricultural production This undermines food security and rural livelihoods. Climate change is intensifying these droughts by increasing their frequency, severity, and duration, placing growing pressure on already vulnerable farming systems.
goal_13.content.#12.title: Exposure to agricultural drought
goal_13.content.#12.subtitle: Areas and share of population exposed to agricultural drought events.[footnote:  An area is defined as being exposed to agricultural drought if at least once in the last 39 years more than 30 percent of cropland or pasture is lost due to drought.]
goal_13.content.#12.source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, Hill et al. [reference: WB2]
goal_13.content.#12.visdescription: Share of population exposed to agricultural drought and location of areas exposed to agricultural drought.
goal_13.content.#12.scenes.#0.text: An [emphasis: area] is considered to be exposed to [emphasis: agricultural drought] if more than 30 percent of the cropland or pasture experienced severe drought in at least one year over the past four decades.[footnote:  This is measured using the Agricultural Stress Index (ASI) developed by FAO.[reference: FAO2] [reference: VanHoolst] ] When this threshold is exceeded, agricultural production losses are expected. [emphasis: Land affected by agricultural droughts has more than doubled in the last 40 years.]
goal_13.content.#12.scenes.#1.text: [emphasis: Agricultural drought] is most prevalent in arid and semi-arid regions. In Eswatini, Lesotho, Namibia, and Zimbabwe more than half the population is exposed to drought.
goal_13.content.#12.scenes.#2.text: Almost 1.4 billion people are exposed to [emphasis: agricultural drought][reference: WB2] and droughts can halt or reverse progress in poverty reduction and food security. Beyond agriculture, prolonged droughts also contribute to environmental degradation, forced migration, and heightened social tensions.
goal_13.content.#14.text: [emphasis: Cyclones] are large revolving storms that form over tropical oceans and are also known as hurricanes or typhoons.[footnote: Different names are used for tropical cyclones depending on their location. Over the Atlantic Ocean and East Pacific Ocean, tropical cyclones are commonly called hurricanes. Tropical cyclones that form in the West Pacific Ocean are referred to as typhoons. Those in the Indian Ocean and around Australia are called cyclones.] Climate change causes ocean water temperatures to rise, which causes cyclones to be stronger resulting in heavier rainfall.
goal_13.content.#15.title: Exposure to cyclones
goal_13.content.#15.subtitle: Area and share of population exposed to cyclones of category 2 or higher.[footnote:  The strength of a cyclone is assessed on a scale from 1 to 5 using the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Wind Scale.]
goal_13.content.#15.source: STORM [reference: Bloemendaal1], Hill et al. [reference: WB2]
goal_13.content.#15.visdescription: Share of population exposed to cyclones and location of areas exposed to cyclones
goal_13.content.#15.scenes.#0.text: Tropical [emphasis: cyclones] of category 2 or higher[footnote: Cyclones are measured on the Saffir Simpson scale, which classifies them into five categories (1–5) based on their maximum sustained wind speed. Cyclones with wind speeds over 154 km/h are classified as Category 2 and above and can cause extensive damage.] are associated with extensive damage to buildings and infrastructure.
goal_13.content.#15.scenes.#1.text: The tropical [emphasis: cyclone] Melissa made landfall in Jamaica on October 28, 2025. It was the third most intense [emphasis: cyclone] in the Atlantic Ocean ever recorded, classified as a category 5 strength cyclone with wind speeds up to 300 km/h.[reference: WMO4] The damage to infrastructure and houses was enormous, but due to early warning systems, the number of casualties was limited at 50.[reference: NOAA1][emphasis: ]
goal_13.content.#15.scenes.#2.text: [emphasis: Cyclones] occur over warm tropical oceans exposing people in their proximity. In fact, in many Small Island Developing States (SIDS), the entire population is exposed. Thirty-six small island developing states in the Caribbean and Pacific Ocean have 100 percent of their population exposed to tropical [emphasis: cyclones].[footnote: These are the following small island developing states: Aruba, Anguilla, American Samoa, Antigua and Barbuda, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba, Bahamas, Saint Barthélemy, Bermuda, Barbados, Cuba, Curaçao, Cayman Islands, Dominica, Dominican Republic, Grenada, Guam, Haiti, Jamaica, Saint Kitts and Nevis, Saint Lucia, Saint Martin (French part), Northern Mariana Islands, Montserrat, Mauritius, Niue, Puerto Rico, Sint Maarten (Dutch part), Turks and Caicos Islands, Tonga, Taiwan, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, British Virgin Islands, United States Virgin Islands, Vanuatu, Wallis and Futuna, Samoa. In Fiji more than 99 percent of the population is exposed.]
goal_13.content.#16.text: [emphasis: Heatwaves] are prolonged periods of unusually high temperatures. Heatwaves can impact human health through an increase in the risk of dehydration, heat exhaustion and heatstroke, and worsen cardiovascular and respiratory diseases. This significantly raises mortality especially among older adults and people with pre-existing conditions.[reference: WHO1] Heatwaves can also reduce economic output, as labor productivity, particularly in outdoor activities such as agriculture, construction, and manufacturing, becomes more strenuous. Additionally, heatwaves increase demand for electricity, and puts additional stress on infrastructure, and healthcare systems.[reference: ILO1]
goal_13.content.#17.title: Exposure to heatwaves
goal_13.content.#17.subtitle: Areas and share of population exposed to heatwaves[footnote:   A heatwave is defined as having 5-day average maximum Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature exceeding 33 degrees  ]
goal_13.content.#17.source: World Bank Climate Change Portal, Hill et al. [reference: WB2]
goal_13.content.#17.visdescription: Share of population exposed to heatwaves and location of areas exposed to heatwaves[emphasis: ]
goal_13.content.#17.scenes.#0.text: The impact [emphasis: heatwaves] have on the human body also depends on other factors such as humidity and wind. Instead of using a simple temperature measure, we rely on a measure called the Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT), as it better captures the effect of temperature on the human body. If the WBGT exceeds 33 degrees Celsius, it becomes difficult for healthy humans to maintain a normal body temperature, which leads to increased mortality and hospitalizations. The capacity to do physical work is also halved.[reference: WB2]
goal_13.content.#17.scenes.#1.text: [emphasis: Heatwaves] are mostly prevalent in the tropics. More than three quarters of the global population exposed to heatwaves live in four populous countries in Asia: India, China, Pakistan, Bangladesh.
goal_13.content.#17.scenes.#2.text: Globally, 40 percent of the population is exposed to [emphasis: heatwaves]. The share of people exposed to [emphasis: heatwaves] is especially high in countries around the Equator in Africa, South-East Asia and the Arabian peninsula.
goal_13.content.#18.text: If we overlay exposures to each of the four climate hazards with population density, 4.5 billion people or around 57 percent of the global population is exposed to at least one climate hazard. There is no country in the world with no people exposed to [emphasis: floods],[emphasis: ][emphasis: agricultural droughts], [emphasis: cyclones][emphasis: ]or [emphasis: heatwaves], but the share of people exposed varies from less than one to 100 percent of a country’s population.
goal_13.content.#19.title: 57 percent of global population exposed to climate-related hazards
goal_13.content.#19.subtitle: Share of population exposed to at least one climate-related hazard
goal_13.content.#19.visdescription: Share of population exposed to at least one climate hazard
goal_13.content.#19.textParagraph: The exposure to the climate hazards floods, agricultural droughts, cyclones and heatwaves depends on the geographical location of a country. In 44 countries the entire population is exposed to at least one climate hazard.
goal_13.content.#19.scale_title: Percentage of population exposed to climate-related hazards
goal_13.content.#19.scale_title_pop: Total number of people exposed to climate-hazard
goal_13.content.#19.scale_title_sha: Percentage of population exposed to climate effects
goal_13.content.#19.keyFactLabel: people are exposed to climate-related hazards
goal_13.content.#19.legend_title: Percentage of population exposed to climate effects
goal_13.content.#21.text: Mapping climate-related hazards is only half the task; the other half is understanding people’s vulnerability to such climate hazards. The loss of income or damage to property caused by a heat wave, drought, or flood may be a small inconvenience for some but a long‑term livelihood shock for others. The effect of the climate-related hazard depends on people’s ability and capacity to cope, adapt and recover. In other words, climate risk is shaped not just by exposure, but also by vulnerability.
goal_13.content.#22.text: The World Bank has identified seven dimensions to assess a person’s general level of vulnerability.[footnote:  The seven dimensions of vulnerability are defined based on existing literature as well as data availability.] These dimensions are grouped into physical and economic vulnerability. People’s physical vulnerability is measured by their access to [emphasis: clean water, ]to [emphasis: electricity],[emphasis:  ]and to[emphasis:  services and markets]. Their economic vulnerability is measured by their [emphasis: income, ] (add link to SDG 1), their [emphasis: education], their access to [emphasis: social protection ]and access to financial services ([emphasis: financial inclusion]).[reference: WB2] Individuals are considered highly vulnerable to adverse events generally, if they score below set thresholds on at least one of these seven dimensions. For vulnerability due to income, for instance, people are identified as being highly vulnerable if their income falls below the extreme poverty line of $3 a day.[reference: WB2]
goal_13.content.#23.title: People identified as highly vulnerable
goal_13.content.#23.label: globally in 2024[reference: WB1]
goal_13.content.#24.text: More than 1 in 3 people globally are considered highly vulnerable in at least one dimension. Many people even experience vulnerability across several of these  dimensions. Let’s look at who is vulnerable according to these measures of vulnerability.[footnote:  We restrict ourselves to the six dimensions of the vulnerability that come from household surveys. The seventh dimension is the access to roads and markets, which is measured using spatial data. As it is not possible to combine the spatial data with the survey data, it is not possible to assess the overlap of these dimensions with the six others. However, the spatial vulnerability measure affects only 44 million out of the total number of 2.8 billion vulnerable worldwide.]
goal_13.content.#25.title: Vulnerability to climate hazards
goal_13.content.#25.subtitle: Number of people vulnerable by dimension, 2024
goal_13.content.#25.keyFactLabel: people are highly vulnerable to shocks
goal_13.content.#25.scenes.#0.text: There are 1,000 squares in each column and each individual square represents 8 million people. More than a third of the[emphasis: ][emphasis: global population], or 2.8 billion people, are vulnerable in at least one dimension. Almost half of them are vulnerable along two or more dimensions. Lack of social protection is the most common source of vulnerability, with 20 percent of the global population facing it. Lack of access to financial services, lack of education, and living in extreme poverty are the next leading causes of vulnerability.
goal_13.content.#25.scenes.#1.text: The level of vulnerability ranges from 1.2 to 93 percent across regions. In [emphasis: North America], only 1.7 percent of the population are identified as being vulnerable. These are people living in extreme poverty or lacking basic education.
goal_13.content.#25.scenes.#2.text: In [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa,] a staggering 94 percent of the population is vulnerable. Lack of social protection impacts 3 out of 4 people in the region and lack of access to electricity or extreme poverty affects half the population.
goal_13.content.#25.scenes.#3.text: Select a country or region to see the share of population that is vulnerable and which dimensions of vulnerability are driving it.
goal_13.content.#26.text: 1.5 billion people are at high risk of climate-related hazards
goal_13.content.#27.text: When we combine exposure to climate-related hazards with vulnerabilities, we can identify people [emphasis: at high risk from climate-related hazards]. People are considered at high risk when they are exposed to any of the four climate-related hazards and are considered highly vulnerable.
goal_13.content.#28.title: At high risk of climate-related hazards
goal_13.content.#28.label: People globally are both exposed [emphasis: and][emphasis:  ]vulnerable to climate-related hazards.
goal_13.content.#29.title: The number of people at high risk from climate-related hazards differs greatly by region
goal_13.content.#29.subtitle: Population shares defined as being at high-risk from climate-related hazards in 2024
goal_13.content.#29.visdescription: The visualization shows the share of regional populations defined as being at high risk of climate-related hazards in 2024.
goal_13.content.#29.scenes.#0.text: On this map, the size of the grey circles is proportional to the size of the population of the seven World Bank regions. The blue circle shows the size of the population exposed to climate-related hazards. The red circle shows the size of the population both exposed and vulnerable to climate-related hazards.
goal_13.content.#30.text: A little under a fifth of the global population are considered at high risk from climate-related hazards, that is they are at high risk of being adversely affected or unable to cope with the impacts. But this does not mean that 18 percent of the population of each country are at high risk. In North America less than one percent of the population (approximately 0.6 million people) are at high risk. In contrast, more than a third of the people living in the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan and Pakistan and Sub-Saharan Africa regions are at high risk.
goal_13.content.#31.title: Population at high risk depends on both vulnerability and exposure
goal_13.content.#31.subtitle: Comparing countries with similar levels of exposure or vulnerability
goal_13.content.#31.keyFactLabel: people are both exposed and highly vulnerable to climate-related hazards
goal_13.content.#31.scenes.#0.text: [emphasis: Burkina Faso ]and [emphasis: Kenya ]have similar levels of [emphasis: vulnerability]: in both countries around 9 in 10 people  are identified as vulnerable in at least one dimension.
goal_13.content.#31.scenes.#1.text: But the [emphasis: exposure ]to climate hazards is three times higher in Burkina Faso. Combining the number of people vulnerable with those exposed, only 1 in 4 people in Kenya are at risk from climate hazards, compared with 3 in 4 people in Burkina Faso.
goal_13.content.#31.scenes.#2.text: In the East Asia and Pacific region, the populations of the [emphasis: Philippines ]and [emphasis: Vietnam] are similarly [emphasis: exposed ]to climate hazards. In both countries almost 9 out of 10 people are exposed.
goal_13.content.#31.scenes.#3.text: However, despite similar [emphasis: exposure ]profiles, slightly more than half the people are at risk in the Philippines, while the same is true for around 20 percent of the Vietnamese population. This is due to the differing levels of vulnerability. In the Philippines, 3 out of 5 people are vulnerable, while the same is true for only 1 in 5 people in Vietnam.
goal_13.content.#32.text: Share of people at high risk from climate-related hazards has halved since 2010
goal_13.content.#33.text: The number of people exposed to climate-related hazards increased from 4.0 billion people to 4.6 billion people between 2010 and 2024. This increase is mainly due to population growth and an increasing number of people living in areas exposed to climate hazards. Yet, during the same time, the number of people identified as being highly vulnerable to unexpected shocks has decreased from 4.4 billion in 2010 to 2.8 billion in 2024.
goal_13.content.#34.text: As a result, the global population at high risk from climate hazards has decreased from 2.6 billion to 1.5 billion between 2010 and 2024.
goal_13.content.#35.text: In only 14 years, the share of the global population who are at high risk from climate-related hazards has halved, from 36 percent in 2010 to 18 percent in 2024. The substantial decrease in the number of people vulnerable shows that to reduce the population at high risk in the short term, governments must improve people’s ability to adapt to the changing climate and increase their resilience.
goal_13.content.#36.text: While almost all regions have successfully reduced the population at risk by reducing the number of people who are highly vulnerable, differences in progress across countries and regions are still stark.
goal_13.content.#37.title: The number of people at high risk from climate-related hazards globally has almost halved since 2010
goal_13.content.#37.subtitle: Population shares defined as being at high-risk from climate-related hazards in 2010 and 2024
goal_13.content.#37.visdescription: The visualization shows the change in the share of global and regional populations defined as being at high risk of climate-related hazards between 2010 and 2023. The map shows that all regions have experienced a decrease in the share at high risk, due to decreasing vulnerability.
goal_13.content.#37.scenes.#0.text: On this map, the size of the grey circles is proportional to the size of the population of the seven World Bank regions. The blue circle shows the size of the population exposed to climate-related hazards. The red circle shows the size of the population both exposed and vulnerable to climate-related hazards.
goal_13.content.#37.scenes.#1.text: By 2024, both total populations and the number of people exposed to climate hazards increased in several regions. However, the [emphasis: share of people at high risk] still declined in most regions because the number of highly vulnerable people significantly reduced. In the East Asia and Pacific region, the highly vulnerable population dropped sharply from 1.3 billion in 2010 to 350 million in 2024. South Asia saw a similar trend, with the number declining from 946 million to 378 million over the same period. In contrast, Sub-Saharan Africa experienced an increase: the number of highly vulnerable people rose from 290 million in 2010 to more than 450 million in 2024.
goal_13.content.#38.title: The number of people at high risk from climate-related hazards has almost halved since 2010
goal_13.content.#38.subtitle: Population shares defined as being at high-risk from climate-related hazards in 2010 and 2023
goal_13.content.#38.visdescription: The visualization shows the change in the share of global and regional populations defined as being at high risk of climate-related hazards between 2010 and 2023. The map shows that all regions have experienced a decrease in the share at high risk, due to decreasing vulnerability.
goal_13.content.#38.textParagraph: In many regions in 2010, most of those exposed to climate hazards were also highly vulnerable. As a result, large shares of the populations were at high risk from climate hazards. By 2024, both total populations and the number of people exposed to climate hazards increased in several regions. However, the [emphasis: share of people at high risk] still declined in most regions because the number of highly vulnerable people significantly reduced. In East Asia and the Pacific, the highly vulnerable population dropped sharply from 1.3 billion in 2010 to 350 million in 2024. South Asia saw a similar trend, with the number declining from 946 million to 378 million over the same period. In contrast, Sub-Saharan Africa experienced an increase: the number of highly vulnerable people rose from 290 million in 2010 to more than 450 million in 2024.
goal_13.content.#40.text: Shocks caused by climate-related hazards can be a threat to income, food security, health, and safety, deepening existing inequalities. Reducing vulnerabilities across seven diverse dimensions and increasing resilience towards a multitude of climate-related hazards can be a daunting task. To help governments prioritize across climate adaptation initiatives, the World Bank has developed the [emphasis: Adaptation and Resilience Readiness Assessment Tool], a whole‑of‑economy diagnostic built around six adaptation principles. Anchored in rapid and inclusive development, the framework focuses on four priority areas: supporting adaptation by firms and people, adapting land use and protecting critical assets, strengthening people’s ability to cope with and recover from shocks, and managing macroeconomic and fiscal risks. Effective governance and continuous monitoring underpin all actions.
goal_13.content.#41.text: These principles, first applied in 17 Caribbean countries, have since guided adaptation efforts elsewhere. In [emphasis: Zambia ]and [emphasis: Brazil], for instance, World Bank–supported programs helped shift from reactive to proactive resilience building, contributing to substantial declines in vulnerability and the number of people at high risk from climate-related hazards.
goal_13.content.#42.text: Experiences such as the ones from Zambia and Brazil show that reducing climate risk is possible, even as exposure continues to increase. While almost one in five - or 1.5 billion - people globally remains at high risk in 2024, this is a remarkable improvement from two in five - or 2.6 billion - in 2010. Continued investments in adaptation, resilience, and inclusive development can further reduce vulnerabilities and protect lives and livelihoods from shocks, including climate‑related shocks.
goal_13.references.Bloemendaal1: Bloemendaal, N., Haigh, I. D., de Moel, H., Muis, S., Haarsma, R. J., & Aerts, J. C. (2020). Generation of a Global Synthetic Tropical Cyclone Hazard Dataset Using STORM. Scientific data, 7(1), 40. doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-0381-2
goal_13.references.CECS: Center for Energy and Climate Solutions

goal_13.references.FAO1: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (n.d.). Drought and agriculture.
goal_13.references.FAO2: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (n.d.). Agricultural Stress Index System (ASIS)
goal_13.references.ILO1: International Labour Organization (2019). Working on a Warmer Planet: The Impact of Heat Stress on Labour Productivity and Decent Work. Geneva: ILO.
goal_13.references.IPCC2023: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC). 2022. Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability. Contribution of Working Group II to the Sixth Assessment Report. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/
goal_13.references.NOAA1: United States National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. (n.d.) Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale
goal_13.references.NOAA2: United States National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration
goal_13.references.NOAA3: United States National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration
goal_13.references.Rentschler: Rentschler, J., Salhab, M. & Jafino, B.A. (2022). Flood exposure and poverty in 188 countries. Nat Commun 13, 3527 https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-30727-4
goal_13.references.Sweet: Sweet et al. (2022). Global and Regional Sea Level Rise Scenarios for the United States. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.
goal_13.references.UN1: United Nations Atlas of the Oceans

FAO’s AVHRR-based Agricultural Stress Index System (ASIS) for global drought monitoring. International Journal of Remote Sensing, 37(2), pp.418-439. https://doi.org/10.1080/01431161.2015.1126378
goal_13.references.WMO1: World Meteorological Organization. 2025. WMO confirms 2024 as warmest year on record at about 1.55°C above pre-industrial level.
goal_13.references.WMO2: World Meteorological Organization. (2025). Climate Change and Workplace Heat Stress.
goal_13.references.WMO3: World Meteorological Organization. WMO confirms 2025 was one of the three warmest years on record.
goal_13.references.WMO4: World Meteorological Organization. (2025). Category 5 Hurricane Melissa hits Jamaica and Caribbean islands.
goal_13.references.WBGT: Budd, G.M. (2008). Wet-bulb globe temperature (WBGT)—its history and its limitations. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport, Volume 11, Issue 1, pp.20-32. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsams.2007.07.003
goal_13.references.WB1: World Bank. (2024). Rising to the Challenge: Climate Adaptation and Resilience. Washington, DC: World Bank.
goal_13.references.WB2: Hill, R., Gorgulu, N., Brunckhorst, B., Nguyen, M., Hallegatte, S., & Naikal, E. (2025). One-fifth of the world’s population at high risk from climate-related hazards (Preprint). Research Square.
goal_13.references.WB3: World Bank. Poverty and Climate Change: Reducing the Vulnerability of the Poor through Adaptation. https://documents.worldbank.org/en/publication/documents-reports/documentdetail/534871468155709473
goal_13.references.UNEP1: United Nations Environment Program. (2020). How climate change is making record-breaking floods the new normal
goal_13.references.UNEP2: United Nations Environment Program. (). Impacts of summer 2003 heat wave in Europe
